steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a transitions question. The previous sentence states that recent research shows tulip mania was "neither as frenzied nor as disastrous as has been thought." The blank introduces the conclusion that the popular myth should be viewed skeptically — a direct result of that research finding.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — for example,: Signals that this sentence illustrates the previous one. But skepticism about the myth is not an example of the research — it is a consequence of it. ✗
  • Choice B — by contrast,: Signals opposition between two ideas. This sentence does not oppose the previous sentence; it follows from it. ✗
  • Choice C — nevertheless,: Signals that something is true despite an obstacle. Nothing here is concessive — the skepticism flows directly from the research. ✗
  • Choice D — therefore,: Signals a cause-and-effect relationship. Because research contradicts the myth, the myth deserves skepticism. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
